nodes.label: Learning with Exact Invariances in Polynomial Time
nodes.source_file: raw/Learning with Exact Invariances in Polynomial Time.pdf
nodes.summary: Soleymani, Tahmasebi, Jegelka, Jaillet (2025). First polynomial-time algorithm for exactly G-invariant kernel regression on Riemannian manifolds, achieving the same minimax-optimal excess risk O(n^{-s/(s+d/2)}) as unconstrained KRR while running in poly(n, d, log|G|) instead of Omega(n^2|G|).

nodes.label: Finite group G acting smoothly and isometrically on manifold M
nodes.source_file: raw/Learning with Exact Invariances in Polynomial Time.pdf
nodes.summary: Invariance setting is a general finite group G (not just rotations) acting smoothly and isometrically on a compact boundaryless d-dim Riemannian manifold M via theta: G x M -> M. Target regression function f* satisfies f*(gx)=f*(x) for all g in G. Example target: permutation group P_d with |G|=d! which is super-exponential in d and why naive group averaging costs Omega(n^2 |G|).

nodes.label: Theorem 1: poly(n, d, log|G|) algorithm with minimax-optimal risk
nodes.source_file: raw/Learning with Exact Invariances in Polynomial Time.pdf
nodes.summary: For f* in Sobolev H^s(M) with s > d/2 and alpha := 2s/d: algorithm runs in O(log^3|G| n^{3/(1+alpha)} + n^{(2+alpha)/(1+alpha)}) time, achieves excess risk R(hat f) = O(n^{-s/(s+d/2)}), requires O(log|G| n^{2/(1+alpha)} + n^{(2+alpha)/(1+alpha)}) oracle calls, and each test-point evaluation costs O(n^{1/(1+alpha)}). First exact-invariance algorithm with polylog dependence on |G|.

nodes.label: Laplace-Beltrami eigenbasis reformulation
nodes.source_file: raw/Learning with Exact Invariances in Polynomial Time.pdf
nodes.source_location: Section 1 pp.1-2, Section 3 oracles, Section 5 proof sketch
nodes.summary: Key technical move: because the Laplace-Beltrami operator commutes with every isometric G-action on M, one can pick a basis of Laplacian eigenfunctions phi_{lambda,ell} so that each g acts on eigenspaces by orthogonal matrices. The nonconvex invariant-kernel-regression problem decomposes into an infinite collection of finite-dimensional convex quadratic programs with linear constraints (one per eigenspace). Truncating the eigen-cut yields the poly-time estimator.

nodes.label: Group averaging baseline is intractable for large |G|
nodes.source_file: raw/Learning with Exact Invariances in Polynomial Time.pdf
nodes.source_location: Section 3, p.3, K_inv definition and complexity discussion
nodes.summary: Naive solutions (data augmentation, group averaging K_inv(x1,x2) = (1/|G|) sum_g K(gx1,x2), canonicalization, frame averaging) either cost Omega(n |G|) or Omega(n^2 |G|), or suffer discontinuities that break RKHS requirements. This motivates needing an exact-invariance algorithm whose runtime is polylogarithmic in |G|.

nodes.label: Eigenfunction evaluation and shifted inner-product oracles
nodes.source_file: raw/Learning with Exact Invariances in Polynomial Time.pdf
nodes.summary: Algorithm assumes two oracles: (1) point-evaluate any eigenfunction phi_{lambda,ell}(x); (2) compute L2(M) inner products <phi_{lambda,ell}(gx), phi_{lambda,ell'}(x)> for any g in G. Both are free as long as the cumulative eigenfunction count D_lambda is poly(n,d). Natural for spheres S^{d-1} (spherical harmonics), Stiefel manifolds, and tori.

nodes.label: Polynomial-time / polylogarithmic-in-|G| complexity regime
nodes.source_file: raw/Learning with Exact Invariances in Polynomial Time.pdf
nodes.summary: The statistical-computational trade-off achieved sits in P: running time is poly(n, d, log|G|). Enumerating G (which can be super-exponential in d, e.g. d!) is provably unnecessary for statistically efficient exact-invariant estimation under Sobolev priors. This is in contrast to Omega(n^2 |G|) group averaging and super-exponential data augmentation.
